items.evidence_text:
Client 1: Combined gross is about seventy thousand eight hundred a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that net? Like after taxes?
Advisor: Good catch—when I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Does the $70,800 sound like the before-tax number?
Client 1: Yes, before tax. About $70,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing you up, Margaret you’re Client 1 and David you’re Client 2, right?

Advisor: Perfect. Now, on the spending side—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Not just bills, like groceries and kid stuff too.
Client 2: Uh, like five grand? Maybe a little under.
Client 1: It’s more like $5,250 a month. I wish it was five flat.
Advisor: Okay—$5,250 per month total expenses. When you say expenses, does that include the mortgage payment, or are you thinking of that separately?
Client 2: I thought expenses meant everything, including the mortgage.

Client 2: Yeah, that’s fine.
Advisor: So for the debt piece—what’s the monthly mortgage payment you’re actually paying right now?
Client 1: $1,750 a month.
Client 2: It’s $1,700—
Client 1: David, no, it’s $1,750. You’re thinking of before escrow went up.

Advisor: Great. Now I want to understand what you’ve got working for you. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so cash, brokerage, retirement accounts, that kind of thing—total?
Client 2: Two hundred thousand-ish? Maybe like 210?
Client 1: Total investable is about $207,650. That’s the number I have.
Advisor: Okay, $207,650 investable assets total. And just to make sure we’re aligned—when you say investable, you’re not counting the house value in that, right?
Client 1: Correct, not the house.

Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: Okay. Going back to the house for a minute—Margaret, David, what would you say the home is worth today, roughly?
Client 1: About $251,100. Like, call it two-fifty-one-ish.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to $260k?
Client 1: No—David, that was the neighbor’s sale. Ours is about $251,100.

Advisor: Yep, payment we already got. I mean the balance, like what you’d see as “principal remaining.”
Client 2: Oh. Sorry—yeah, I mix that up. I think it’s like $150k?
Client 1: It’s $157,300 outstanding. I pulled it up earlier.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. So mortgage outstanding balance is $157,300.
Advisor: And just to be super clear, that $157,300 is basically your total loans outstanding as a household, right? Or do you have anything else—car loan, student loans, credit cards carrying a balance?

Advisor: Yep, payment we already got. I mean the balance, like what you’d see as “principal remaining.”
Client 2: Oh. Sorry—yeah, I mix that up. I think it’s like $150k?
Client 1: It’s $157,300 outstanding. I pulled it up earlier.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. So mortgage outstanding balance is $157,300.
Advisor: And just to be super clear, that $157,300 is basically your total loans outstanding as a household, right? Or do you have anything else—car loan, student loans, credit cards carrying a balance?

Client 1: Combined gross is about seventy thousand eight hundred a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that net? Like after taxes?
Advisor: Good catch—when I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Does the $70,800 sound like the before-tax number?
Client 1: Yes, before tax. About $70,800 gross for the household.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing you up, Margaret you’re Client 1 and David you’re Client 2, right?

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi David—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what made you decide to talk now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, it’s the monthly squeeze—kids, mortgage, all of it. I want a plan that’s not just vibes.
Client 2: Yeah, and I want to make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Margaret tracks it all, I just—try to keep up.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to that monthly squeeze you mentioned, Margaret—can we start with your overall household income, gross per year, roughly?
Client 1: Combined gross is about seventy thousand eight hundred a year.

Client 1: David, no, it’s $1,750. You’re thinking of before escrow went up.
Client 2: Oh—right, yeah, sorry. $1,750.
Advisor: No worries—escrow changes trip everyone up. I’m writing down monthly debt cost total as $1,750. Any other loans or payments we should include in that debt bucket, or is it basically just the mortgage?
Client 1: Basically just the mortgage.
Advisor: Great. Now I want to understand what you’ve got working for you. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so cash, brokerage, retirement accounts, that kind of thing—total?

Client 2: Yeah, not like California or New York. Just… other.
Advisor: Okay, I’ll mark residence state as “Other.”
Advisor: Now, risk tolerance—when we talked earlier you both sounded pretty growth-leaning, but I want to put a word on it. Would you say conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I mean, we’ve got four kids—
Client 1: Aggressive. Not reckless, but aggressive for the long-term stuff.

Client 1: Yes, record it as aggressive.
Client 2: Yeah—okay, aggressive, but with guardrails.
Advisor: Perfect. One more that trips people up sometimes: tax bracket. Do you know roughly what bracket you land in—like 22%, 24%, 32%—that kind of thing?
Client 2: Is that like… what we get back at tax time? Because last year we got a refund—
Client 1: No, that’s not—sorry—he means the marginal bracket. We’re in the 32% bracket.

Advisor: Okay. Let me pivot to the rest of what you own—just so I’ve touched each bucket at least once.
Advisor: You mentioned investable assets total about $207,650. Can we break that into the main accounts? Like taxable cash/bank, retirement, and anything else.
Client 1: Sure. The big one is our joint taxable at the bank—about $102,400.
Client 2: That’s the one we call the “buffer,” yeah.
Advisor: Okay, joint taxable bank account around $102,400.

Client 1: U.S., yes.
Client 2: Yeah, US.
Advisor: Great. And the “market” label I use is basically the channel—like a U.S. registered investment advisor setup. Does “US RIA” sound right for how we’re doing this?
Client 2: Uh—RIA like… the advisor model? Yeah, that sounds right.
Client 1: Yes, US RIA.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi David—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what made you decide to talk now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, it’s the monthly squeeze—kids, mortgage, all of it. I want a plan that’s not just vibes.
Client 2: Yeah, and I want to make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Margaret tracks it all, I just—try to keep up.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to that monthly squeeze you mentioned, Margaret—can we start with your overall household income, gross per year, roughly?

Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier about the mortgage feeling like the “don’t mess with this” bill—before we move on, I want to lock a couple household basics in place.
Client 1: Okay.
Advisor: First one’s simple: Margaret, David—are you two legally married, or is it a domestic partnership/civil partnership situation?
Client 2: Married.
Client 1: Yep, married.

Advisor: And then the house we already covered—primary residence value about $251,100.
Client 2: Right.
Advisor: On the income side—just so I’m not mixing sources—earlier we had household gross income around $70,800 a year. Margaret, your gross is about $56,550, and David yours is about $14,200, right?
Client 2: Yeah, I’m around $14,200 gross.
Client 1: And I’m $56,550 gross, yes.

Advisor: I hear you. I’ll keep it moving.
Advisor: One more detail on you, Margaret—what’s your date of birth? Month, day, year is fine.
Client 1: October 28th, 1986.
Client 2: I thought it was the 27th—
Client 1: No, it’s the 28th. You always do that.

Client 1: Yeah, that’s in my name. About $23,750.
Advisor: Got it—$23,750 in a brokerage private markets holding.
Advisor: And then the house we already covered—primary residence value about $251,100.
Client 2: Right.
Advisor: On the income side—just so I’m not mixing sources—earlier we had household gross income around $70,800 a year. Margaret, your gross is about $56,550, and David yours is about $14,200, right?

Advisor: Yeah, good catch to ask, David. Gross is before taxes; net is what hits the checking account.
Client 2: Okay—yeah, I always mix that up.
Advisor: Totally normal. And Margaret, you’re currently employed, correct?
Client 1: Yes, I’m employed.
Advisor: David, same for you—employed as well?

Advisor: Operations—got it.
Client 2: She basically keeps the place running.

Client 1: Yeah, it does. It’s assured until June 4th, 2046.
